summary: Simon Späti argues that universal data orchestrators are the essential 'heartbeat' of modern data engineering, addressing challenges like complexity management, heterogeneous architectures, data quality, and governance. He traces the evolution from cron jobs to declarative YAML-based orchestrators, emphasizing the convergence of infrastructure-as-code principles with data pipeline management. The post advocates for centralized orchestration over implicit event-driven approaches for complex data workloads, highlighting Kestra's dual interface paradigm (UI + Code) as a bridge between technical and business users. Späti concludes that modern orchestrators must support software engineering best practices while remaining approachable to non-programmers.
key_points: Data orchestrators solve four core challenges: complexity management, handling heterogeneous architectures, continuous data quality/error handling, and data governance
The evolution from cron to modern orchestrators reflects a shift toward declarative, version-controlled pipelines that support software engineering best practices
Centralized orchestration is preferable over implicit event-driven approaches for complex data workloads because they require end-to-end visibility and governance
The dual interface paradigm (UI + Code) bridges the gap between technical users who need version control and business users who need approachability
YAML has become the lingua franca of modern system orchestration, enabling declarative configuration across DevOps and data engineering
money_quotes: The orchestrator is the heart of any data engineering project, potentially any data platform.

Data workloads are different. These are less time-critical and usually vastly complex with upstream data out of our control; we need to implement hack upon hack, and need to deliver high-quality data to the users.
Without a data orchestrator, you won't have a scheduler, data lineage, visualizer, or handy function to restart a process. You end up with disjointed stored procedures.

Coding is hard, especially for business specialists who need to keep up within their field and domain and just need to get their jobs scheduled. You can't expect them to learn programming on the side.

Generally, software best practices are hard to apply to data engineering. We know that from the history of SSIS and Informatica, GUI-driven tools where you need to click through 100 tables if you want to load tables.

Imagine an airplane; it's vastly complex. No person, not even a pilot, can know the inside out of every jet, every piece. That's why they created a cockpit, with hundreds of lights and knobs to turn and see what's going on.
key_insight: Modern data orchestrators must combine declarative code-first approaches with intuitive UIs to bridge the gap between software engineering best practices and accessibility for business users.
